acasus.name: Acasus
acasus.market: International development consulting for public sector reform (health & education) in LMICs
acasus.market_size: ~$45.5B global education consulting by 2031; LMIC reform niche is a small, donor-funded slice
acasus.position_detail: Founder-built boutique with embedded-delivery model. Competes on field presence and measurable outcomes, not scale.

advantages.label: Embedded delivery model
advantages.detail: 80% local talent, on-the-ground in 10+ countries. Competitors often fly in/fly out.

advantages.label: Track record with proof points
advantages.detail: Punjab vaccination 50% to 90% in 2 years. Quantifiable results that win government trust.

advantages.label: Budapest data engineering hub
advantages.detail: 24-person technical team (AWS, Tableau, ETL). Unusual capability for a development consultancy.

advantages.label: Founder credibility
advantages.detail: Fenton Whelan is a recognized voice in impact consulting. World Health Summit, Harvard Africa Health Conference.

disadvantages.label: Scale limitation
disadvantages.detail: ~265 staff vs thousands at Big 4 / MBB impact practices. Cannot compete on volume bids.

disadvantages.label: Aid-funding dependency
disadvantages.detail: Revenue tied to ODA cycles. US foreign aid contraction is a direct threat to contract pipeline.

disadvantages.label: No product revenue
disadvantages.detail: Pure services model. No recurring SaaS or platform revenue to smooth contract lumpiness.

disadvantages.label: CEO transition risk
disadvantages.detail: Founder stepped back 2025. New CEO still establishing. Relationship-based business depends on leadership continuity.

competitors.name: Palladium
competitors.differentiation: Bigger (5,000+ staff), broader, but less embedded. More program management than reform delivery.

competitors.name: DAI
competitors.differentiation: Larger, US-headquartered, strong USAID relationships. More traditional development contractor.

competitors.name: Oxford Policy Management
competitors.differentiation: More research-oriented. Less operational delivery. Overlaps on evidence-based reform.

competitors.name: McKinsey Social Impact
competitors.differentiation: Brand and scale advantage. But higher cost, shorter engagements, less embedded presence.

competitors.name: BCG Public Sector
competitors.differentiation: Growing impact portfolio. Threatens at the premium end of the market.

metrics.label: Revenue
metrics.context: Mid-range for development boutiques. Growing with headcount.

metrics.label: Headcount
metrics.context: +22% YoY. Small vs Big 4 but large for niche.

metrics.label: Country footprint
metrics.context: Pakistan, Chad, DRC, Mozambique, Somalia, Afghanistan, Ethiopia, Nigeria, Sudan, Bahrain.

metrics.label: Data team size
metrics.context: Unusual technical depth for a development consultancy.

cspd.name: Catholic Schools Parramatta Diocese
cspd.position_detail: Second-largest Catholic diocesan system in NSW, 1-in-4 Western Sydney students. Low-fee, mission-driven, government-funded.

advantages.label: Mission-driven community trust
advantages.detail: Multi-generational Catholic families, parish-school integration, 70+ year presence

advantages.label: Scale and system support
advantages.detail: 80+ schools with centralised ELT, Canvas LMS, Wellbeing Directorate, shared services

advantages.label: Uplift framework
advantages.detail: Evidence-based teaching reform with AERO endorsement and CECG-proven methodology

advantages.label: Growth corridor position
advantages.detail: Located in Australia highest-growth demographic corridor with documented excess demand

disadvantages.label: Operating deficit
disadvantages.detail: -$20.6m in 2023 with locked salary escalation of ~$19m/year under the EA

disadvantages.label: Government funding dependency
disadvantages.detail: 80.7% government-sourced revenue, politically exposed post-2029

disadvantages.label: Teacher supply constraint
disadvantages.detail: Sector-wide shortage amplified by RE accreditation barrier unique to Catholic schools

disadvantages.label: Infrastructure lag
disadvantages.detail: 500+ families turned away annually, competing against $7.9B NSW public school building program

competitors.name: NSW Department of Education (government schools)
competitors.positioning: Primary enrolment competitor for all families in CSPD's western Sydney catchment. Government schools
competitors.differentiation: $3.6 billion committed in 2024-25 NSW Budget for new and upgraded schools in western Sydney — record education infrastructure spend, including 24 new schools and 51 upgraded schools | Melonba Public School (north-west Sydney) opened Term 1 2025 ahead of schedule; Melonba High School catering up to 2,000 students — directly in CSPD's Blacktown/Hills growth corridor | New high school for Gledswood Hills/Gregory Hills (south-west Sydney) — temporary campus 2025, permanent campus 2027 — in CSPD's south-west expansion corridor | Upgrades at Leppington and Austral Public Schools (20+ new permanent classrooms each) to meet enrolment demand in areas where CSPD has high LBOTE populations | NSW DoE 2024 NAPLAN messaging: 81% of Year 3 students recording Strong or Exceeding in writing; active counter-narrative to CSPD's NAPLAN positioning

competitors.name: Low-to-mid fee independent schools in western Sydney
competitors.positioning: Fastest-growing enrolment competitor. Since 2000, NSW independent schools have captured almost two-t
competitors.differentiation: Independent sector grew enrolments at 3.3% per year over five years (2020-2025) vs Catholic systemic growth of 1.2% per year | 10 new low-to-mid fee independent schools opened in Sydney's north-west and south-west in the past decade, with many existing schools expanding | More than half of independent school students now come from low-to-medium income households — eroding CSPD's traditional positioning as the accessible Catholic option | Faith-based Islamic and evangelical Christian schools in western Sydney specifically targeting LBOTE families — CSPD's core demographic (53.4% LBOTE) is the key contested segment

competitors.name: Sydney Catholic Schools (SCS / Archdiocese of Sydney)
competitors.positioning: Overlapping catchment competitor in border areas. SCS operates 147 schools in the Archdiocese of Syd
competitors.differentiation: Magis 2033 launched early 2026 with Boston College partnership — elevates SCS's academic credibility and national profile above CSPD's current positioning | School reporting overhaul (Feb 2026) positions SCS as innovating on parent communication — directly addresses a dimension where CSPD has no announced equivalent initiative | 73,853 students, 147 schools vs CSPD's 45,646 / 83 — SCS's scale advantage enables shared services efficiencies and negotiating leverage CSPD cannot match independently | Teacher recruitment: SCS actively recruits in western Sydney suburbs and shares the same EA, meaning CSPD competes directly with SCS for teacher talent in the same labour market

ipeople.name: iPeople Inc
ipeople.market: Philippine private higher education (STEM-focused, dual-tier: premium + affordable)
ipeople.position: Market leader in STEM private HE
ipeople.position_detail: Conglomerate-backed (Yuchengco + Ayala) education holding company. Differentiated by Mapua brand prestige and STEM focus.

advantages.label: Mapua University brand
advantages.detail: Philippines top engineering school. 100+ year legacy. Brand is the primary competitive moat.

advantages.label: Dual-tier pricing
advantages.detail: Premium (Mapua) + affordable (NTC/APEC). Captures both segments of the market.

advantages.label: Conglomerate backing
advantages.detail: Yuchengco Group + Ayala Corporation. Deep pockets for expansion and technology investment.

advantages.label: Public listing (PSE:IPO)
advantages.detail: Capital markets access for growth. Governance discipline from public reporting requirements.

advantages.label: K-12 pipeline
advantages.detail: Own K-12 feeds into HE institutions. Integrated enrollment funnel.

disadvantages.label: Thin margins
disadvantages.detail: 2-3.6% net margin on PHP 5.32B revenue. Little buffer for investment or downturns.

disadvantages.label: Scale gap vs PHINMA
disadvantages.detail: 74,000 students vs PHINMA's 177,851. Half the enrollment, similar market positioning.

disadvantages.label: Dual-parent governance
disadvantages.detail: Joint Yuchengco + Ayala ownership. Strategic alignment risk if parent priorities diverge.

disadvantages.label: Geographic concentration
disadvantages.detail: Primarily Metro Manila and Luzon. Less provincial penetration than PHINMA.

competitors.name: PHINMA Education
competitors.differentiation: 2.4x enrollment. Provincial focus. Lower price point. PE-backed (KKR). Direct threat in affordable segment.

competitors.name: University of Santo Tomas
competitors.differentiation: Prestige brand. But single-campus, not a network operator.

competitors.name: AMA Education System
competitors.differentiation: Overlaps on tech education. Broader geographic spread but lower prestige.

competitors.name: STI Education Systems
competitors.differentiation: More vocational focus. Listed (PSE:STI). Competes in affordable tech education.

metrics.label: Institutions
metrics.context: Across STEM HE, K-12, and blended learning.

metrics.label: Board exam pass rates
metrics.context: Primary quality signal in Philippine HE market.

kinetic.name: Kinetic Software
kinetic.market: Vertical SaaS for higher education operations (accommodation, conferencing, catering, student engagement)
kinetic.market_size: UK PBSA is a 50B+ GBP asset class. 180M+ bed-nights/year under management. Growing internationally.
kinetic.position_detail: 85% UK university penetration. Dominant in home market. Two-player global market with geographic splits.

advantages.label: 85% UK market penetration
advantages.detail: Near-monopoly in home market. APUC #1 framework ranking means Scottish universities can buy without tender.

advantages.label: Deep switching costs
advantages.detail: Embedded in university HR/finance/accommodation workflows with 25+ years of institutional configuration.

advantages.label: Multi-product platform
advantages.detail: Accommodation + conferencing + catering + student engagement. Cross-sell within existing customers.

advantages.label: Data scale
advantages.detail: 700,000+ managed bed-spaces. Aggregate benchmarking data is a competitive advantage.

advantages.label: Domain expertise
advantages.detail: 28 years in HE operations. Generic PropTech cant replicate institutional knowledge overnight.

disadvantages.label: UK saturation ceiling
disadvantages.detail: 85% = near-maximum. Growth must come from ARPU, new modules, or international markets.

disadvantages.label: Small team
disadvantages.detail: ~86 employees. Capacity-constrained for product development, support, and international expansion simultaneously.

disadvantages.label: Geographic concentration
disadvantages.detail: Primary revenue from UK. International expansion is early-stage with different regulatory environments.

disadvantages.label: Platform risk
disadvantages.detail: Generic PropTech (Yardi, RealPage) could move downstream into education. Scale disadvantage if they do.

competitors.name: StarRez
competitors.differentiation: Dominates North America and Australia (1,400 customers, 3M+ beds). The other half of the duopoly. Stronger internationally.

competitors.name: Yardi
competitors.differentiation: Massive scale ($500M+ revenue). Could enter education vertical. But lacks HE domain expertise.

competitors.name: RealPage
competitors.differentiation: Large platform player. Student housing is one vertical of many. Possible competitive threat.

competitors.name: Campus Living Villages
competitors.differentiation: Operator that builds own tools. Vertical integration threat but niche.

metrics.label: Customers
metrics.context: Institutional clients across UK/Europe and growing internationally.

metrics.label: Team size
metrics.context: Efficient but capacity-constrained for growth ambitions.

phinma.name: PHINMA Education
phinma.market: Affordable private higher education in Southeast Asia (Philippines + Indonesia)
phinma.market_size: $154M (2025) growing to $847M by 2034 at 20.23% CAGR (Philippine HE market)
phinma.position_detail: Largest private higher education provider in Southeast Asia by enrollment. Acquisition-and-turnaround model targeting underserved provincial markets.

advantages.label: Scale dominance
advantages.detail: 177,851 students. Largest private HE network in SEA. 20-year head start in acquisition model.

advantages.label: Regulatory moat (PACUCOA Level III)
advantages.detail: Highest accreditation tier. 3-5 year process for new entrants. CHED recognition is mandatory for degree-granting.

advantages.label: Board exam brand flywheel
advantages.detail: #1 Nursing (99.16%), #1 Criminology (92.80%). Results attract students, better students produce better results.

advantages.label: Provincial network density
advantages.detail: 13 campuses embedded in communities across Luzon/Visayas/Mindanao. Geographic switching costs for students.

advantages.label: PE backing (KKR)
advantages.detail: P4.5B from KKR Global Impact Fund. Funds acquisition pipeline and digital transformation mandate.

advantages.label: Cost discipline
advantages.detail: 'Bare-bones, no-frills, brass knuckles' philosophy. 23% net margin is exceptional for education.

disadvantages.label: Faculty pipeline constraint
disadvantages.detail: Structural shortage of licensed healthcare educators in provincial markets. Growth ceiling.

disadvantages.label: Early-stage technology
disadvantages.detail: Basic LMS app. CIO in place but digital maturity lags enrollment scale. KKR mandate to fix this.

disadvantages.label: Indonesia execution risk
disadvantages.detail: 50K student target in 5yr. JV structures (Yayasan) add complexity. Different regulatory environment.

disadvantages.label: Government subsidy dependency
disadvantages.detail: Free HE Act is a material revenue floor. Policy reversal would directly impact affordability model.

competitors.name: iPeople Inc (PSE:IPO)
competitors.differentiation: 74,000 students (42% of PHINMA). Stronger in engineering/STEM. Thinner margins. Metro Manila focused.

competitors.name: AMA Education System
competitors.differentiation: Broader geographic spread. IT/computer science focus. Lower prestige than PHINMA board exam results.

competitors.name: STI Education Systems
competitors.differentiation: Vocational focus. Listed (PSE:STI). Different segment but overlaps in affordable tech education.

competitors.name: University of the Philippines
competitors.differentiation: Free tuition (government-funded). Highly selective. Not a direct competitor for PHINMA target market.

competitors.name: Universitas Ciputra (Indonesia)
competitors.differentiation: Potential competitor in Indonesia expansion. Established brand in entrepreneurship education.

metrics.label: Enrollment
metrics.context: Largest private HE in SEA. +14% YoY revenue growth.

metrics.label: Net margin
metrics.context: P1.5B net income. Exceptional for education sector.

metrics.label: Institutions
metrics.context: 11 Philippines + 2 Indonesia. Pipeline: 4 more Philippine cities.

metrics.label: Retention rate
metrics.context: High for affordable segment. Each 1% = ~1,780 students.

metrics.label: Nursing pass rate
metrics.context: Key brand metric. Self-reinforcing quality signal.

rising.name: Rising Academies
rising.market: Affordable education delivery and AI tutoring in Sub-Saharan Africa
rising.market_size: $7.3B (2025) growing to $19.2B by 2034 (SSA education market). 250M+ out-of-school children globally.
rising.position_detail: Evidence-led, B-Corp certified education social enterprise. Differentiates on RCT-validated learning outcomes and WhatsApp-native AI tutoring.

advantages.label: Published RCT evidence
advantages.detail: arXiv:2402.09809 shows 0.36 SD effect size. One of very few SSA operators with peer-reviewed impact evidence.

advantages.label: WhatsApp-native AI (Rori/Tari)
advantages.detail: 150,000+ users. Zero friction distribution via WhatsApp. Works in low-bandwidth environments.

advantages.label: Government partnerships
advantages.detail: 4 national MoUs (Sierra Leone, Liberia, Ghana, Rwanda). Institutional relationships are a high-switching-cost moat.

advantages.label: B-Corp + mission alignment
advantages.detail: Certified B-Corp. Avoids the political backlash that hits pure-profit school chains in Africa.

advantages.label: Data network effect
advantages.detail: Rori AI improves with each interaction. Compounding accuracy advantage over new entrants.

disadvantages.label: Not yet profitable
disadvantages.detail: Grant-subsidized. $2-8M budget dependent on philanthropic and impact capital. Revenue sustainability unproven.

disadvantages.label: Quality at 900+ sites
disadvantages.detail: 300,000+ students across 4 countries. Consistency challenge as operations outpace infrastructure.

disadvantages.label: Government payment risk
disadvantages.detail: B2G contracts = political risk + payment delays. Budget cycles in SSA are unreliable.

disadvantages.label: Narrow geographic base
disadvantages.detail: West Africa concentrated. Expansion to East/Southern Africa would require new government relationships.

competitors.name: Bridge International Academies
competitors.differentiation: Larger scale but controversial. Political backlash in Kenya, Uganda. Rising's evidence-led positioning is a direct counter.

competitors.name: NewGlobe
competitors.differentiation: Government partnership model similar to Rising. Larger operations. Less AI-focused.

competitors.name: Eneza Education
competitors.differentiation: SMS/app-based tutoring. Overlaps on mobile delivery but less school-level integration.

competitors.name: Ubongo
competitors.differentiation: Content/media approach vs Rising's school operations. Different model, similar mission.

competitors.name: Anthropic / ChatGPT
competitors.differentiation: Rising partnered with Anthropic for Rori. But generic AI chatbots could disintermediate if WhatsApp integration improves.

metrics.label: RCT effect size
metrics.context: Published, peer-reviewed. Rare in the sector.

school-for-life.name: School for Life Foundation
school-for-life.market: International education charity (Australian fundraising, Ugandan delivery)
school-for-life.market_size: Australian international development charity sector. ~$1.4B total DGR donations to international causes (2024).
school-for-life.position_detail: Small, deep, locally-led model. Competes for Australian philanthropic dollars against other education charities and broader international development causes.

advantages.label: 17-year track record
advantages.detail: Founded 2008. Longevity builds donor trust. Holistic model (primary + secondary + vocational) over full lifecycle.

advantages.label: Founder personal brand
advantages.detail: Annabelle Chauncy OAM. Keynote circuit (Saxton/Halogen). Personal credibility drives major donor relationships.

advantages.label: Premium donor events
advantages.detail: Gala Ball is a high-touch, exclusive experience. Attracts HNW individuals in Sydney finance/real estate.

advantages.label: Locally-led model
advantages.detail: 250 staff in Uganda, majority local. Authenticity narrative resonates with donors. 43 acres of owned land.

advantages.label: Dramatically better student-teacher ratio
advantages.detail: ~18:1 vs Uganda national average of ~50:1 (government schools). Clear differentiation.

disadvantages.label: Revenue concentration
disadvantages.detail: Gala Ball + small number of major donors = high concentration risk. One bad year could be material.

disadvantages.label: Scale ceiling
disadvantages.detail: 10 schools, 4,500 students. Growth constrained by fundraising capacity, not demand.

disadvantages.label: FX exposure
disadvantages.detail: Revenue in AUD, costs in UGX. Currency movements directly impact operational budget.

disadvantages.label: Dual-geography management
disadvantages.detail: ~30 Australia + 250 Uganda. Coordination overhead for a small organization.

competitors.name: One Girl
competitors.positioning: Australian charity for girls education in Africa
competitors.differentiation: Similar donor base (Australian women). Different model (scholarships vs school operation). Competes for same philanthropic dollars.

competitors.name: Room to Read
competitors.differentiation: Massive scale (39 countries). Better known internationally. More institutional funding.

competitors.name: PEAS (Promoting Equality in African Schools)
competitors.positioning: UK charity operating schools in Uganda
competitors.differentiation: Direct overlap: charity-run schools in Uganda. Larger (35 schools). UK donor base, not Australian.

competitors.name: Opportunity International
competitors.differentiation: Different model (microfinance for school fees). Some donor base overlap in Australia.

metrics.label: Schools
metrics.context: Primary + secondary + vocational in rural Uganda.

metrics.label: Students
metrics.context: Deep engagement per student over full education lifecycle.

metrics.label: Land owned
metrics.context: Physical asset base in Uganda. Rare for education charity.

metrics.label: Est. annual revenue
metrics.context: Gala Ball + major donors + recurring giving.

wise.name: WISE
wise.market: Global education innovation convening and policy influence
wise.market_size: Niche. Global education summit/convening space is not a traditional market. Value is measured in policy influence, not revenue.
wise.position_detail: Qatar Foundation-backed global platform. Unique positioning at intersection of Gulf soft power, education innovation, and Global South policy.

advantages.label: Qatar Foundation sovereign backing
advantages.detail: ~$405M/yr QF total spend. Removes financial pressure competitors face. Can invest in long-term programming.

advantages.label: Sheikha Moza patronage
advantages.detail: UNESCO Special Envoy for Basic and Higher Education. Structurally unreplicable personal endorsement.

advantages.label: WISE Prize prestige
advantages.detail: $500K prize called 'Nobel Prize for Education.' Key brand asset. 12,000+ cumulative summit attendees.

advantages.label: Global South focus
advantages.detail: Differentiates from Western-centric convenings (OECD, World Bank). Attracts diverse policymaker audience.

advantages.label: 15+ year brand equity
advantages.detail: 35,000+ community members. 150,000+ engaged followers. Network effects in policy influence.

disadvantages.label: Single-funder dependency
disadvantages.detail: Qatar Foundation = ~100% of operational funding. Strategic misalignment with QF leadership is existential.

disadvantages.label: Tiny team
disadvantages.detail: ~36 FTE. Cannot sustain year-round programming, deep research, and community engagement simultaneously.

disadvantages.label: Impact measurement gap
disadvantages.detail: Convening ROI is hard to prove. Relies on soft metrics (attendees, media mentions) vs hard outcomes.

disadvantages.label: Geopolitical perception risk
disadvantages.detail: Gulf-state backing can be perceived as soft power tool. May affect credibility with some Western institutions.

competitors.name: UNESCO
competitors.differentiation: Institutional authority and global mandate. But bureaucratic, slow-moving. WISE is more agile.

competitors.name: World Bank Education
competitors.differentiation: Massive funding and research capacity. But focused on lending, not innovation convening.

competitors.name: ASU+GSV Summit
competitors.differentiation: US-centric, commercially focused. WISE is policy-focused, Global South-oriented.

competitors.name: HundrED
competitors.differentiation: Innovation focus overlaps. Smaller. Nordic credibility. Less policy clout.

competitors.name: OECD Education
competitors.differentiation: Data authority (PISA). Western-focused. WISE differentiates on practitioner engagement and Global South.

metrics.label: QF backing
metrics.context: WISE share undisclosed. Operational stability guaranteed.
